title: ContextInjector::inject ignores allowlist; system messages from disallowed hooks still injected
description:
## Severity
Major
## Dimension
security
## Location
src/context.rs:88-119 (ContextInjector::inject)
## Violation
```rust
pub fn inject(request: &mut ChatCompletionRequest, hook_result: &HookResult) {
    let system_messages: Vec<&str> = hook_result.system_messages();
    if system_messages.is_empty() { return; }
    let combined = system_messages.join("\n\n");
    let reminder = wrap_system_reminder(&combined);
    ...
}
```

The injector checks neither `hook_result.allowed` nor `hook_result.errors`. A hook that explicitly denied the operation (`allowed = false`) but still produced an output is treated identically to a passing one — its `system_message` field is injected into the next turn's user message, silently. This couples failed authorization with passed prompt context.

Worse, when a hook's `system_message` includes attacker-controlled content sourced from a tool's output (file content, web fetch result), the only defense is the delimiter-neutralization escape — there is no other sanitization, no length cap, and no content-type validation. A 10 MB web-fetch result inserted into a hook output ships verbatim into the model's user message.
## Mandated refactor
Add an explicit `if !hook_result.allowed { return; }` guard, OR change the API to take a `&PermittedHookResult` newtype that can only be constructed from an allowed result. Add a per-hook output length cap (e.g. 32 KiB after envelope) and surface oversize-truncation in the injected text. Document in the function doc-comment that hook outputs are routed through the model — and that hooks therefore must NEVER include unsanitized tool output.
## Filed from QA review dispatch
